responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia project sits at the intersection of deep phenotyping and scalable methods validation. Core tensions include achieving sufficient ecological validity through residential monitoring while maintaining safety, cost discipline, and efficient data handling; and balancing human expert scoring with semi-automated triage to reduce manual review burden without sacrificing accuracy. The chosen levers push toward a staged facility expansion, multimodal sensing, diversified but controlled recruitment, robust data governance, incremental automation with clinician oversight, and diversified funding. This combination addresses data richness, generalizability, and sustainability while preserving ethical safeguards and the potential for reproducible event-triage tools.

levers.name: Facility footprint optimization and expansion planning
levers.consequences: Altering the footprint directly changes enrollment capacity and operating risk. Expansion to twelve suites increases throughput but raises capital costs and staffing requirements; maintaining eight suites keeps costs and risk lower but may limit data yield; reallocating space toward data infrastructure prioritizes measurement quality with potential throughput constraints.
options: Expand to twelve suites with phased staffing and revised safety protocols to support longer stays.
Maintain eight suites and optimize throughput through scheduling and workflow improvements.
Reallocate space toward data infrastructure and equipment while keeping eight suites to maximize data quality per participant.
levers.review_lever: This lever centers on the tension between scale and safety and cost. The options miss a staged, compliance-first expansion pathway that could reduce upfront risk.

levers.name: Sensing modality strategy
levers.consequences: Choosing the sensing approach determines data richness and operating burden. A mixed protocol balances data quality with participant burden and cost, but requires robust data integration. Full PSG on all nights maximizes resolution but increases workload, staffing, and potential participant fatigue.
options: Adopt a mixed-sensor protocol with nightly dry-electrode EEG, contact-free sensors, and episodic PSG on key nights.
Move toward continuous full PSG montages on all nights to maximize data fidelity.
Limit monitoring to low-burden dry-electrode headbands and infrared cameras to maximize throughput.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances data richness versus participant burden and cost, but the options miss a unified data-integration plan that ensures cross-modality harmonization.

levers.name: Recruitment strategy optimization
levers.consequences: Recruitment strategy directly shapes cohort size and representativeness. Broadening recruitment accelerates enrollment and improves external validity but increases coordination and screening burden; hospital-centric recruitment simplifies workflows but may limit participant diversity and generalizability; adaptive enrollment provides responsiveness but risks timeline drift.
options: Broaden recruitment through regional sleep clinics and community partnerships with enhanced participant compensation.
Concentrate recruitment within hospital clinics to streamline consent and ensure high data quality.
Implement adaptive enrollment targets with interim performance reviews and external recruitment consultants.
levers.review_lever: This lever weighs breadth of access against operational simplicity, but the options miss a plan to ensure diversity and data quality across sites.

levers.name: Data governance and privacy posture
levers.consequences: Data governance determines collaboration potential and participant privacy. Tight privacy controls protect participants but slow external collaboration; a data enclave enables controlled access with governance but increases overhead; synthetic data sharing improves openness but reduces granularity.
options: Enforce strict de-identification and access controls with a formal data-use agreement framework.
Establish a controlled data enclave offering tiered access to external researchers under governance.
Publish synthetic data summaries to share patterns while preventing re-identification.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances privacy with collaboration, but the options overlook standardized data-sharing templates across partners. Without an implementation template, external collaborations may stall.

levers.name: Event-triage development strategy
levers.consequences: Semi-automated triage strategies influence review throughput and accuracy. A semi-automated tool with clinician adjudication can raise efficiency but requires robust training data; maintaining dual-human scoring preserves reliability but slows progress; cross-site validation expands generalizability but may delay deployment.
options: Develop and validate a semi-automated triage tool with clinician adjudication on all flagged events.
Maintain dual-human scoring as gold standard while progressively integrating automation.
Pilot cross-site validation with external dataset to test generalizability before deployment.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances automation with clinical oversight, but the options miss a plan for incremental field testing in a single-site pilot to calibrate thresholds.

levers.name: Funding strategy and governance
levers.consequences: Funding strategy governs sustainability and speed of progress. Milestone-based funding aligns resources with progress but risks schedule slippage; pharma collaboration preserves publication rights but may invite perceived conflicts; diversified funding reduces financial risk but can complicate governance.
options: Institute milestone-based funding releases with external review triggers to enforce accountability.
Negotiate enterprise-style collaboration with pharma while preserving publication rights.
Pursue diversified funding from public grants and philanthropic sources to reduce reliance on industry.
levers.review_lever: This lever embeds financial discipline with scholarly freedom, but the options miss an integrated framework for long-term self-sufficiency beyond grant cycles.

responses.summary: Add 'regional sleep-clinic referral partnerships with standardized pre-screening' to Recruitment Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The plan must operationalize a residential research unit that balances rigorous data quality, participant safety, and ethical constraints with achievable throughput. Key tensions include maintaining a naturalistic environment while ensuring staff wellbeing, protecting privacy without crippling data utility, and delivering timely, multi-modal annotations with human-in-the-loop oversight. The proposed levers push beyond single-focus optimizations by integrating safety, privacy, automation, recruitment efficiency, data integrity, and external validation, enabling robust phenotyping and method validation while preserving the plan’s safety, ethical, and methodological guardrails.

levers.name: Overnight staffing model optimization for eight-suites operations
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reconfigures night-shift coverage around eight active suites, aiming to improve incident response and safety monitoring. It can reduce response delays during parasomnia events but may raise labor costs and staff fatigue if rotations are too aggressive. Downstream, it influences training demands, scheduling complexity, and morale, with potential impacts on retention and coverage reliability.
options: Recalibrate night-shift staffing to align coverage with peak parasomnia events while maintaining safety, training, and burnout mitigation.
Institute a tiered responder model where on-site staff initiate remote escalation to clinical teams for high-severity events, improving response consistency.
Experiment with a rotating on-call cross-coverage system that pairs experienced staff with newer technicians to accelerate learning and maintain safety.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between safety coverage and personnel workload, but the options overlook transition costs and onboarding time for cross-training. Additionally, it risks uneven coverage if turnover is high.

levers.name: Privacy-preserving video handling and consent-for-video workflow
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever defines how video data are captured, stored, and accessed, making privacy safeguards central to research utility. Privacy-preserving configurations can reduce participant concerns and regulatory risk, but may constrain certain analytical approaches or require additional infrastructure and workflow complexity. Downstream, the need for consent administration and data access governance can affect recruitment speed and data sharing timelines.
options: Implement a consent-first, minimum-identity video capture framework with selective blurring and local pre-screening to protect privacy.
Adopt a tiered access model for video data where de-identified clips are shared for research while raw footage remains bound by strict approvals.
Design an opt-in video logging protocol that collects baseline data without recording sensitive surroundings, balancing transparency and data utility.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between privacy safeguards and data utility, but the options miss downstream considerations around consent administration and data access costs. Operational costs and consent management burdens are not addressed.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage tool integration with dual scorer feedback loop
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever integrates a semi-automated event triage prototype with the existing scoring workflow, aiming to reduce manual review burden while preserving accuracy. It may shift annotation throughput and timing, potentially freeing scorer time but requiring robust validation and calibration to prevent drift. Downstream, improvements in efficiency could accelerate research outputs but increase dependency on algorithmic components and cross-site data alignment.
options: Integrate a semi-automated event triage prototype that flags potential parasomnia episodes and routes them to dual scorers for adjudication.
Establish a calibration study to align algorithmic rankings with human scoring across diverse participant profiles.
Pilot a feedback loop where scorer disagreements retrain the triage model to improve accuracy over time.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances automation and human adjudication, but the options overlook calibration drift and scorer fatigue. It also assumes consistent scorer availability, which may be challenged during extended pilot phases.

levers.name: Pre-admission screening enrichment via structured telehealth intake
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever shapes the pre-admission path by expanding screening and collateral history collection, potentially improving participant safety and eligibility clarity. It can reduce late-stage screening delays but may introduce new administrative overhead and access barriers for participants with limited connectivity. Downstream, this could affect enrollment pace and data quality from the outset.
options: Institute a structured telehealth intake that expands collateral history collection and risk assessment before admission.
Pilot a standardized exclusion checklist interview to reduce dropouts and improve data quality from the outset.
Incorporate home-based pre-assessments that streamline admission scheduling and minimize late-stage screening delays.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets pre-admission filtering, but the options ignore potential access barriers for participants with limited telehealth connectivity. It also risks delaying enrollment if telehealth adds steps.

levers.name: Longitudinal data integrity and artifact handling strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever emphasizes data quality across multi-modal streams and longitudinal collection, improving comparability and analysis readiness. It can enhance data reliability, but may increase upfront protocol design complexity and validation overhead. Downstream, it may affect timelines for initial publications if data cleaning becomes more rigorous.
options: Adopt a standardized artifact labeling workflow and cross-site synchronization checks to preserve data quality across modalities.
Implement automated artifact detection with human-in-the-loop review to maintain accuracy without overburdening scorers.
Establish a data reconciliation cadence that resolves mismatches between sensor streams within a defined weekly window.
levers.review_lever: This lever focuses on data quality and interoperability, but the options overlook governance for legacy data and version control. Implementing version control and provenance tracking will be necessary to avoid drift over time.

levers.name: External validation and replication pathways with phase-two planning
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever institutionalizes external credibility and cross-site replication, supporting generalizability and public trust. Coordinating across centers introduces logistical complexity and potential delays in publishing, but can broaden the evidence base and accelerate progress toward scalable methods validation. Downstream, successful replication could influence funding and partnership opportunities but requires harmonized protocols and data ownership agreements.
options: Design a phased external validation plan that engages external sleep centers to replicate methods and benchmark performance.
Draft a phase-two proposal outline and metrics to secure funding for multi-site replication and intervention testing.
Create a collaborative publication strategy that coordinates shared data and methods while preserving participant confidentiality.
levers.review_lever: This lever strengthens external credibility, but the options overlook alignment of protocols across sites and data ownership. It also risks inconsistent data governance across sites if standards are not harmonized.

responses.summary: Add 'structured telehealth pre-admission checklist with collateral history verification' to Pre-admission screening enrichment via structured telehealth intake.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project operates at the convergence of safe residential monitoring, high-quality data capture, participant recruitment efficiency, and rigorous methods validation. Core tensions arise from balancing staged physical expansion with data-quality milestones, maintaining ecological realism without compromising safety, delivering timely event adjudication with inter-rater reliability, protecting privacy while enabling external validation, and coordinating cross-site collaboration without bureaucratic drag. The six levers herein directly address capacity governance, safety oversight, adjudication reliability, living-environment realism, data governance, and external validation—providing disciplined levers to de-risk expansion, improve reproducibility, and accelerate scholarly output while preserving participant trust and regulatory compliance.

levers.name: Staged capacity expansion tied to data quality milestones
levers.consequences: Phased capacity expansion ties new suites to data-quality milestones and operational readiness. This prioritizes proven processes and reduces risk of unutilized space, but delays achieving full footprint and slows participant accrual.
options: Phase the addition of sleeping suites based on achieving predefined annotation throughput and acceptable inter-rater reliability across two independent scorers.
Implement a staged expansion trigger requiring successful pilot data quality milestones before leasing or renovating additional suites.
Adopt a flexible occupancy policy that proceeds with expansion only after safety drills and staffing saturation tests meet qualitative thresholds.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances the tension between rapid capacity growth and ensuring data-quality controls. The options miss transition costs and scheduling disruptions that could slow recruitment and data accrual.

levers.name: Enhanced remote safety oversight and nurse presence
levers.consequences: Adds remote monitoring dashboards and on-call safety oversight during nighttime observation. This expands safety coverage without constant on-site presence, but relies on robust communication workflows and may increase on-call workload and overhead.
options: Deploy real-time remote monitoring dashboards with standardized escalation protocols and on-call nurse response during nighttime observation.
Reassign sleep technicians to hybrid on-site and remote oversight roles to optimize coverage and reduce fatigue.
Integrate rapid-response safety drills and emergency signaling to minimize reaction times during nocturnal events.
levers.review_lever: This lever highlights the tension between safety coverage breadth and on-site presence. The options may miss dependencies on reliable communication infrastructure and potential increases in on-call burdens.

levers.name: Structured event adjudication pipeline with cross-site calibration
levers.consequences: Improves reliability and consistency of event scoring through a calibrated adjudication workflow. The trade-off is a potential reduction in throughput and a need for dedicated calibration time and training.
options: Establish a tiered adjudication workflow with cross-site calibration sessions and weekly review of discordant cases.
Introduce a learning loop where discrepant cases trigger targeted retraining for scorers and standardization audits.
Incorporate external blinded raters for select events to improve scoring reliability and reduce scorer drift.
levers.review_lever: Core tension lies between timely results and scoring reliability; the options may overburden staff and slow progress if calibration cycles are too frequent.

levers.name: Naturalistic environment optimization balancing safety and ecological validity
levers.consequences: Enhances ecological validity and participant comfort by preserving a domestic feel while enabling rapid isolation when needed. The trade-off is increased management overhead and potential data variability across environments.
options: Redesign living spaces with modular safety features that preserve domestic ambience while enabling rapid isolation in emergencies.
Tune ambient conditions, lighting, and noise to reduce participant stress and improve data quality without compromising comfort.
Adopt flexible protocol nights that adjust monitoring intensity based on real-time safety feedback and participant tolerance.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between ecological realism and uniform safety controls; the options risk introducing night-to-night variability that could complicate analyses.

levers.name: Data handling and privacy framework with tiered access control
levers.consequences: Establishes privacy-first governance with de-identification, access-tiering, and audit trails before any external sharing. This strengthens compliance and trust but may constrain granularity and slow external collaboration.
options: Institute a privacy-first data governance plan with de-identification, access-tiering, and audit trails prior to any external sharing.
Develop a local data enclave with controlled transfer to university storage and strict access authentication.
Publish de-identified, aggregated datasets with licensing clarity to enable external validation while preserving participant privacy.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is openness versus privacy; the options may introduce bottlenecks in data retrieval for timely analyses.

levers.name: Cross-institutional collaboration for methods validation and replication
levers.consequences: Expands external validity and accelerates dissemination by validating methods across centers and sharing workflows. This requires alignment on ethics, governance, and harmonized protocols, which can introduce coordination overhead.
options: Form formal joint protocols with external sleep centers to replicate key findings and share validated workflows.
Coordinate multi-center pilot studies using harmonized sensor setups to benchmark event-triage tools across sites.
Establish shared repositories for annotated datasets and scoring guidelines to accelerate validation and peer review.
levers.review_lever: Core tension exists between centralized coordination and local autonomy; the options may face bureaucratic delays and policy discrepancies across institutions.

responses.summary: Add 'external blinded rater calibration sessions quarterly' to Structured event adjudication pipeline.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Example: "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
